request.question: What was the name of the 1929 novel written by the author whose scenario inspired the one-act ballet "Le Train Bleu"?

title: Le Train Bleu (restaurant)
Blue Train Races
Bar aux Folies-Bergère (ballet)
Picasso and the Ballets Russes
Le Train de Nulle Part
Jean Cocteau
Pep Bertran
Hidden Agenda (video game)
Le Train Bleu
Le Train Bleu (ballet)
sentences: Le Train Bleu ("The Blue Train") is a restaurant located in the hall of the Gare de Lyon railway station in Paris, France.
It was designated a "Monument Historique" in 1972.
The Blue Train Races were a series of record-breaking attempts between automobiles and trains in the late 1920s and early 1930s.
It saw a number of motorists and their own or sponsored automobiles race against the "Le Train Bleu", a train that ran between Calais and the French Riviera.

The rationale to beat the train was to compare the contemporary automotive performance with locomotive dominance; to showcase recent progress achieved by cars regarding reliability, durability, speed and comfort; to promote the cars, their marques and the adventurous persona of their drivers; and to establish automobiles as a viable and aspirational mode of transport for the individual traveller.
Bar aux Folies-Bergère is a one-act ballet created in 1934, with scenario and choreography by Ninette de Valois, and designs by William Chappell after Manet.
The music consists of piano works by Emmanuel Chabrier, selected and arranged by Constant Lambert.
Pablo Picasso and the Ballets Russes collaborated on several productions.

Pablo Picasso's Cubist sets and costumes were used by Sergei Diaghilev in the Ballets Russes's "Parade" (1917, choreography: Léonide Massine), "Le Tricorne" ("The Three-Cornered Hat") (1919, choreography: Massine), "Pulcinella" (1920, choreographer: Massine), and "Cuadro Flamenco" (1921, choreography: Spanish folk dancers).
Picasso also drew a sketch with pen on paper of "La Boutique fantasque" ("La course"), (1919, choreography: Massine).
and designed the drop curtain for "Le Train Bleu" (1924, choreography: Bronislava Nijinska), based on his painting, "Two Women Running on the Beach" ("The Race"), 1922.
Le Train de Nulle Part (The Train from Nowhere) is a 233-page French novel, written in 2004 by a French doctor of letters, Michel Dansel, under the pen name Michel Thaler.
Notable as an example of constrained writing, the entire novel is written without a single verb.
Jean Maurice Eugène Clément Cocteau (] ; 5 July 1889 – 11 October 1963) was a French writer, designer, playwright, artist and filmmaker.
Cocteau is best known for his novel "Les Enfants Terribles" (1929), and the films "The Blood of a Poet" (1930), "Les Parents Terribles" (1948), "Beauty and the Beast" (1946) and "Orpheus" (1949).

His circle of associates, friends and lovers included Kenneth Anger, Pablo Picasso, Gertrude Stein, Jean Hugo, Jean Marais, Henri Bernstein, Yul Brynner, Marlene Dietrich, Coco Chanel, Erik Satie, Albert Gleizes, Igor Stravinsky, Marie Laurencin, María Félix, Édith Piaf, Panama Al Brown, Colette, Jean Genet, and Raymond Radiguet.
Pep Bertran (born in Mataró, Maresme, 20 September 1971) is a Catalan writer.
He studied information science, psychoanalysis and film direction.
In 1996 he received the "Premi de novel·la curta Just Manuel Casero" Award for his novel "La Ribera", which was published in 1997 by Empúries publishing house.
Then, he left the writing and he was trained as a psychoanalyst.
He collaborated on "El Punt" newspaper between 1997 and 1998.
His novel "Una vida regalada", the first of the "Narratives" collection (Edicions Saldonar) is a fake autobiography of author whose scenario is La Ribera of Barcelona.

Hidden Agenda is a 1988 computer strategy game with menu-driven interface and early, black and white graphics whose scenario was designed and written by Jim Gasperini, with input from project consultant Eric Ehrmann.
While the scenario was implemented in text form, the game made use of an innovative graphical interface, with naturalistic characters, settings, and digital video interstitials.
The narrative simulation system was designed and implemented by Greg Guerin and Ron Martinez (who also produced the game).
Ron Martinez also designed and implemented the front end user experience, in the process inventing one of the first implementations of digital video with frames grabbed from a hacked four-head VCR.
The game was intended to simulate the conditions of a post-revolutionary Central American country.
It is considered a forerunner of the Games for Change movement, alongside other early Macintosh games including Chris Crawford's "Balance of Power".
The Calais-Mediterranée Express was a luxury French night express train which operated from 1886 to 2003.
It gained international fame as the preferred train of wealthy and famous passengers between Calais and the French Riviera in the two decades before World War II.
It was colloquially referred to as Le Train Bleu in French (which became its formal name after World War II) and the Blue Train in English because of its dark blue sleeping cars.
Le train bleu is a one-act ballet choreographed by Bronislava Nijinska to music by Darius Milhaud for Serge Diaghilev's Ballets Russes, based on a scenario by Jean Cocteau.
The title was taken from the night train called Le Train Bleu, which transported wealthy passengers from Calais to the Mediterranean Sea.
The ballet is set at the fashionable resort of Deauville and has a sporting theme, with swimmers, tennis players, and weight lifters.
Henri Laurens supplied a Cubist beach scene and Coco Chanel outfitted the cast in sportswear.
The curtain was painted after "Deux femmes courant sur la plage", a 1922 work by Pablo Picasso.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: What was the name of the 1929 novel written by the author whose scenario inspired the one-act ballet "Le Train Bleu"?
hop1_docs: Le Train Bleu (ballet) | Le train bleu is a one-act ballet choreographed by Bronislava Nijinska to music by Darius Milhaud for Serge Diaghilev's Ballets Russes, based on a scenario by Jean Cocteau.  The title was taken from the night train called Le Train Bleu, which transported wealthy passengers from Calais to the Mediterranean Sea.  The ballet is set at the fashionable resort of Deauville and has a sporting theme, with swimmers, tennis players, and weight lifters.  Henri Laurens supplied a Cubist beach scene and Coco Chanel outfitted the cast in sportswear.  The curtain was painted after "Deux femmes courant sur la plage", a 1922 work by Pablo Picasso.

Le Dieu bleu | Le Dieu bleu is a ballet in one act choreographed by Michel Fokine to music by Reynaldo Hahn, set to a libretto by Jean Cocteau and Federico de Madrazo y Ochoa.  Léon Bakst designed the sets and costumes.  The ballet was a failure at the premiere in Paris, on 13 May 1912 at the Théâtre du Châtelet.  "Le Dieu bleu" was staged three times in Paris in 1912, three times in London in 1913, and revived in London in April 2011 with music cobbled together from the works of Scriabin and with choreography by Wayne Eagling.  The revival was not a success.

The Gas Heart | The Gas Heart or The Gas-Operated Heart (French: "Le Cœur à gaz" ) is a French-language play by Romanian-born author Tristan Tzara.  It was written as a series of non sequiturs and a parody of classical drama—it has three acts despite being short enough to qualify as a one-act play.  A part-musical performance that features ballet numbers, it is one of the most recognizable plays inspired by the anti-establishment trend known as Dadaism.  "The Gas Heart" was first staged in Paris, as part of the 1921 "Dada Salon" at the Galerie Montaigne.

L'Oiseau Bleu (train) | L'Oiseau Bleu (or the Oiseau Bleu) was an international express train linking Antwerp with Paris.  The train was named after the play "L'Oiseau Bleu" as a tribute to its author, the Belgian Nobel prize laureate Maurice Maeterlinck.

The Blue Dahlia (ballet) | The Blue Dahlia (French: "Le Dahlia bleu" ) is a fantastic ballet in two acts, with libretto and choreography by Marius Petipa and music by Cesare Pugni, first presented by the Imperial Ballet on May 12 [O.S. April 30] 1860 at the Imperial Bolshoi Kamenny Theatre in St. Petersburg, Russia, with Mariia Surovshchikova-Petipa (as the Blue Dahlia) and Timofei Stukolkin (as Beausoleil).

Le Pavillon d'Armide | Le Pavillon d'Armide is a ballet in one act and three scenes choreographed by Michel Fokine with music by Nikolai Tcherepnin on a libretto by Alexandre Benois.  It was inspired by the novella "Omphale" by Théophile Gautier.

Le Train Bleu (restaurant) | Le Train Bleu ("The Blue Train") is a restaurant located in the hall of the Gare de Lyon railway station in Paris, France.  It was designated a "Monument Historique" in 1972.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Jean Cocteau — author of the Le Train Bleu scenario.

- Concrete facts from passages:
  - Passage [1]: "based on a scenario by Jean Cocteau"
  - Passage [1]: "one-act ballet"
  - Passage [1]: Le Train Bleu is choreographed by Bronislava Nijinska (contextual fact)

- Missing / ambiguous:
  - Missing fact: "title of the 1929 novel by Jean Cocteau" (bridge entity: Jean Cocteau). No novel title or 1929 date appears in the provided passages.

- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives:
  1. Fetch "Jean Cocteau" (Wikipedia) — inspect lede/infobox and the "Works", "Bibliography", or "Novels" section for publications listed in 1929.
  2. Fetch "Jean Cocteau bibliography" or "List of works by Jean Cocteau" pages/sections.
  3. Fetch or search for "Les Enfants terribles (novel)" (possible candidate page — verify publication year and authorship).
hop2_docs: Jean Cocteau | Jean Maurice Eugène Clément Cocteau (] ; 5 July 1889 – 11 October 1963) was a French writer, designer, playwright, artist and filmmaker.  Cocteau is best known for his novel "Les Enfants Terribles" (1929), and the films "The Blood of a Poet" (1930), "Les Parents Terribles" (1948), "Beauty and the Beast" (1946) and "Orpheus" (1949).  His circle of associates, friends and lovers included Kenneth Anger, Pablo Picasso, Gertrude Stein, Jean Hugo, Jean Marais, Henri Bernstein, Yul Brynner, Marlene Dietrich, Coco Chanel, Erik Satie, Albert Gleizes, Igor Stravinsky, Marie Laurencin, María Félix, Édith Piaf, Panama Al Brown, Colette, Jean Genet, and Raymond Radiguet.

Thomas the Impostor | Thomas the Impostor (French: "Thomas l'imposteur" ) is a 1964 French film directed by Georges Franju and starring Emmanuelle Riva, Fabrice Rouleau, Sophie Dares, Jean Marais and Charles Aznavour.  It is based on a novel of the same name by Jean Cocteau, which had first been published in French in 1923 and had been inspired by Cocteau's experiences in the first World War.  An American edition of the novel, translated into English by Lewis Galentiere, was published in 1925 under the title "Thomas the Impostor", and another English translation by Dorothy Williams was subsequently published in 1957 under the title "The Impostor".  The film was entered into the 15th Berlin International Film Festival.

Les Enfants Terribles | Les Enfants Terribles is a 1929 novel by Jean Cocteau, published by Editions Bernard Grasset.  It concerns two siblings, Elisabeth and Paul, who isolate themselves from the world as they grow up, an isolation which is shattered by the stresses of their adolescence.  It was first translated into English by Samuel Putnam in 1930 and published by Brewer & Warren Inc.  A later English translation was made by Rosamond Lehmann in 1955, and published by New Directions (ISBN  ) in the U.S., and Mclelland & Stewart in Canada in 1966, with the title translated as "The Holy Terrors".  The book is illustrated by the author's own drawings.

Les Enfants Terribles (film) | Les Enfants Terribles ("The terrible children") is a 1950 French film directed by Jean-Pierre Melville and based on Jean Cocteau's novel of the same name.  The first feature film of Melville, "Le Silence de la Mer" (1949), attracted the attention of Jean Cocteau, who commissioned him to direct the film version of "Les Enfants Terribles".

Orpheus (film) | Orpheus (French: Orphée ; also the title used in the UK) is a 1950 French film directed by Jean Cocteau and starring Jean Marais.  It is the central part of Cocteau's Orphic Trilogy, which consists of "The Blood of a Poet" (1930), "Orpheus" (1950) and "Testament of Orpheus" (1960).

Marie-Laure de Noailles | Marie-Laure de Noailles, Vicomtesse de Noailles (] ) (31 October 1902 – 29 January 1970) was a French artist, regarded one of the 20th century's most daring and influential patrons of the arts, noted for her associations with Salvador Dalí, Balthus, Jean Cocteau, Ned Rorem, Man Ray, Luis Buñuel, Francis Poulenc, Wolfgang Paalen, Jean Hugo, Jean-Michel Frank and others as well as her tempestuous life and eccentric personality.  She and her husband financed Ray's film "Les Mystères du Château de Dé" (1929), Poulenc's "Aubade" (1929), Buñuel and Dalí's film "L'Âge d'Or" (1930), and Cocteau's "The Blood of a Poet" (1930).

Jean Cocteau Museum | The Jean Cocteau Museum/Séverin Wunderman Collection is a museum in Menton, on the French Riviera, in the Alpes-Maritimes department.  Dedicated to the French artist Jean Cocteau, it incorporates the collection of American businessman and Cocteau enthusiast Séverin Wunderman.
response.summary_2: The 1929 novel was Les Enfants Terribles. Jean Cocteau was a French writer (1889–1963).
